text: The Necessity of a Unified Framework for LLM Agentic Capabilities Evaluation Report GitHub Issue × Title: Content selection saved. Describe the issue below: Description: Submit without GitHub Submit in GitHub arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! Learn more × Back to arXiv Why HTML? Report Issue Back to Abstract Download PDF Abstract 1 Introduction 2 Sources of Variance Beyond the LLM 2.1 Inference Configuration Inference Interfaces and Protocols. Inference Engine Variability. 2.2 Prompting and Planning Strategies Prompt Scaffolding. Planning Strategies. 2.3 Memory Mechanisms Memory Formatting. Memory Management Under Length Constraints. 2.4 Tool Invocation Tool Schema Choices. Parameter Type Support. Parser Robustness for Non-Native Tool Calls. 2.5 External Environments 3 The Necessity of a Unified Framework 3.1 Analogies from Other Benchmarking Communities Wind tunnel testing. GPU benchmarking. Motorsport regulation. 3.2 Two Components a Unified Framework Must Provide A controlled execution substrate. A principled evaluation methodology. 4 What a Unified Framework Should Specify 4.1 Standardized Dataset Composition 4.2 The Harness Layer 4.3 The Evaluation Methodology 4.4 Governance Explicit versioning. Open process and empirical anchoring. 5 Alternative Views “Traditional LLM Benchmarks Already Capture Agentic Capability” “Standardization Slows Innovation in Agent Research” “Existing Work Already Solves This” “Use Production Agents like Claude Code or Codex Instead” “MCP and Similar Protocol Efforts are Sufficient” “Sandboxed Environments Sacrifice Ecological Validity” References A Prompt Design Comparison: BFCL vs. smolagents A.1 BFCL system prompt A.2 smolagents system prompt (abridged) Smolagents system prompt. A.3 Discussion B Memory Representation: smolagents vs. Benchmark Harnesses B.1 smolagents: structured MemoryStep objects B.2 τ \tau -bench: linear chat history B.3 BFCL: minimal scaffolding by design B.4 Implications for cross-harness comparison C When the Live Environment Drifts: Three Trajectory-Level Case Studies C.1 Case 1: Stale Snippet, Confidently Wrong C.2 Case 2: Anti-Bot Denial of the Only Authoritative Source C.3 Case 3: Silent Search-Index Drift C.4 Why these are measurement problems, not model problems License: CC BY 4.0 arXiv:2602.03238v2 [cs.AI] 26 May 2026 The Necessity of a Unified Framework for LLM Agentic Capabilities Evaluation Pengyu Zhu 1 Li Sun 1 Philip S. Yu 2 Sen Su 1,3† 1 Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications 2 University of Illinois Chicago 3 Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications whfelingyu_zhupengyu@bupt.edu.cn Abstract The agentic capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly evaluated through executable benchmarks, in which the LLM is wrapped in an agent harness (prompts, memory, tool interfaces, and parsers) and situated in an execution environment. This system-level structure creates an identification problem: when two LLMs obtain different agent benchmark scores, the difference may reflect agentic capability, but it may also reflect harness compatibility, environment drift, or evaluator-specific conventions. We argue that this problem is no longer a marginal nuisance but a structural barrier to scientifically interpretable progress in LLM agent evaluation. Our position is that cross-LLM evaluation of agentic capability requires a unified evaluation framework that standardizes the non-LLM components while preserving benchmark-specific task content. This position is deliberately narrower than a call to standardize all agents: deployed agent products should continue to innovate in prompts, planners, tools, memory, and interfaces, whereas controlled model evaluation must hold these factors constant to make model-level attribution possible. Building on this distinction, we identify what should be standardized and what should remain free to vary, propose a versioning-based governance model for evolving specifications, and demonstrate feasibility through migration case studies. The goal is to enable fairer, more interpretable, and more reproducible progress in LLM agentic capability evaluation. 1 Introduction As large language model (LLM)–based agents become increasingly prevalent [ 30 ] , evaluating agentic capabilities has emerged as a central criterion for assessing modern LLMs in their deployment as general-purpose agents [ 14 , 23 , 2 ] . Unlike traditional LLMs that exhibit primarily static input–output behavior [ 13 ] , LLM-based agents operate as integrated systems with internal mechanisms for planning and memory, and interact with external environments through tool use [ 33 ] . This system-level nature creates a new identification problem: observed scores depend not only on the LLM under test, but also on how the surrounding components are instantiated. A growing number of agent benchmarks have been proposed, aiming to measure autonomous decision-making [ 11 ] , tool invocation [ 20 ] , and task execution [ 31 ] in interactive environments. Yet each benchmark is typically built on its own bespoke surrounding stack: the non-LLM components that wrap the LLM during evaluation. This stack comprises a harness layer (prompting, memory, and tool-invocation mechanisms) and an execution environment. Because this stack is neither standardized across benchmarks nor reported in detail within any single one, reported scores conflate the LLM’s contribution with decisions made elsewhere in the stack. Recent empirical work reinforces this concern: the Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] demonstrates that the same LLM, evaluated on the same benchmark but under different harnesses, can produce substantially different scores. Without a shared evaluation standard, benchmarks remain incomparable, and improvements cannot be reliably attributed to underlying agentic capability. We therefore argue that cross-LLM evaluation of agentic capability requires a unified evaluation framework that standardizes the non-LLM components while preserving benchmark-specific task content. By a unified framework, we specifically mean a controlled experimental setup in which the agent harness and execution environment are held fixed across models and benchmarks, so that observed performance differences can be attributed to the LLM under test rather than to decisions elsewhere in the stack. This position has two important boundaries. First, a unified framework does not collapse different agent benchmarks into a single benchmark: it standardizes the surrounding stack through which any benchmark is evaluated, while preserving each benchmark’s content and evaluation goals. Second, unification does not aim to freeze innovation in agent design. Our position concerns evaluation-time controlled comparison rather than the design of deployed agent products such as Claude Code [ 1 ] or Codex [ 15 ] , where harness-level innovation is both expected and welcome. These two activities answer different questions—"which LLM contributes more under a fixed harness?" versus "which agent product performs best end-to-end?"—and this paper is concerned with the former. Several recent efforts move in compatible directions but stop short of the position we advocate. The Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] empirically shows that harness variation matters but does not articulate a community- level standardization position. τ \tau -bench [ 32 ] pioneers trajectory-aware evaluation within specific domains. Terminal-Bench [ 10 ] and Harbor [ 5 ] integrate multiple agents in a shared environment but preserve agent-side heterogeneity. Protocol-level efforts such as MCP [ 6 ] standardize tool invocation, addressing one slice of the harness. Our position is complementary: we argue for what should be standardized at evaluation time, where to draw the line between standardization and innovation, and how such a standard can evolve. Figure 1 : Confounded LLM capability measurement. Each layer of the surrounding stack introduces variance independent of the LLM under test. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 analyzes the major sources of variance in existing agent evaluation, showing how harness implementation choices propagate into scores independently of the LLM under test. Section 3 argues that meaningful cross-LLM comparison therefore requires standardizing this surrounding layer, drawing on analogies from mature engineering communities and identifying the two components a unified framework must provide. Section 4 identifies what these components should fix—and what they should leave open to legitimate benchmark variation—together with a versioning-based governance model under which the specification can evolve. Section 5 addresses principled disagreements with our position, including concerns about innovation, the sufficiency of existing efforts, the role of production agents, protocol-level standardization, and ecological validity. 2 Sources of Variance Beyond the LLM Not all variance in LLM agentic capabilities evaluation is equally problematic. Variance from task design, e.g., different benchmarks deliberately providing different tools or environments to test different capabilities, is part of what is being measured and should be preserved. Variance from non-LLM components is incidental to the capability under test, yet directly confounds the assessment of LLM agentic capability (see Figure 1 ). Examples include one provider’s safety filter versus another’s, one researcher’s system prompt scaffold versus another’s, one harness’s memory format versus another’s, one schema convention versus another’s, and one evaluation’s live web versus another’s snapshot. This section concerns the latter category, analyzing each layer in turn: inference configuration, prompting and planning, memory, tool invocation, and the execution environment. 2.1 Inference Configuration Inference configuration governs both decoding behavior and the execution interface through which models are invoked, yet is rarely standardized across evaluation setups. Inference Interfaces and Protocols. Even with the same model and identical prompts, provider-specific protocols can produce divergent execution. Content moderation differs across providers: identical prompts may execute through the OpenAI API but be filtered or rejected through Google’s GenAI interface, where stricter safety thresholds apply [ 24 ] . Provider-level request handling can also cause hard execution failures unrelated to agentic capability—requests through Azure OpenAI [ 12 ] may fail under content policy enforcement 1 1 1 e.g., “The response was filtered due to the prompt triggering Azure OpenAI’s content management policy.” while the identical prompt succeeds via OpenAI’s native API. From the standpoint of evaluation, such failures are indistinguishable from agent incompetence. Inference protocols additionally impose interface-specific constraints on tool definitions and function schemas, which we discuss in Section 2.4 . Inference Engine Variability. In open-source settings, the same model weights executed under different engines—vLLM [ 8 ] , SGLang [ 36 ] , HuggingFace Transformers [ 29 ] —can exhibit divergent behavior owing to engine-specific implementation differences [ 35 ] . Such differences are largely tolerable in single-turn LLM evaluation but become consequential in agent settings, where small divergences at any decision point can propagate into different tool calls and divergent trajectories. 2.2 Prompting and Planning Strategies While modern LLMs increasingly carry agentic behaviors learned during post-training, system prompts and planning scaffolds still substantially shape how those capabilities are surfaced at evaluation time. Prompts specify tool-use formats, action constraints, and planning procedures; planning scaffolds determine whether and how an agent decomposes tasks, reflects on errors, or replans. Different choices on either dimension can produce different evaluation outcomes for the same model. Prompt Scaffolding. A natural objection is that prompts must be benchmark-specific—a function-calling benchmark needs a function-calling prompt; a customer-service benchmark needs a customer-service persona. We agree, and treat such task-defining content as part of the benchmark itself. The variance we are concerned with is the surrounding scaffold: the procedural and reasoning structure layered on top of the task description. As Appendix A illustrates, two harnesses evaluating the same kind of capability can ship scaffolds an order of magnitude apart in size—BFCL’s ∼ \sim 200-word prompt fixes only an output format, while smolagents’ [ 22 ] ∼ \sim 2,000-word scaffold additionally encodes an Action/Observation loop, in-context worked examples, an explicit facts-survey-and-plan procedure, and a replanning step. Reporting both as “the model’s tool-use score” silently attributes scaffold quality to the model. Planning Strategies. Contemporary agent systems have largely converged on ReAct [ 33 ] as the default paradigm, with earlier alternatives such as Chain-of-Thought [ 28 ] and Plan-and-Execute [ 26 ] now occupying narrower roles, and native function-calling APIs [ 16 , 4 ] offering an orthogonal interface for structured tool calls. Active research layers further reasoning structures on top—reflection, self-critique, tree-of-thought search—and these scaffolds can substantially improve task performance. Such scaffold-level innovations and a model’s own agentic capability are two distinct evaluation targets, and conflating them obscures both. Even within a single paradigm, differences in planning granularity, action constraints, and reflection mechanisms can systematically alter trajectories, so cross-LLM comparison demands holding the scaffold fixed. 2.3 Memory Mechanisms Memory enables coherence across interactions and adaptation based on accumulated experience [ 30 ] . In an LLM agent, memory is largely a harness-level construct: how past content is formatted, retained across truncation boundaries, and retrieved across episodes is decided by the surrounding framework. The model itself contributes only the in-context attention applied over whatever the framework chooses to expose. Memory Formatting. Different harnesses serialize trajectories, tool calls, and outcomes in different ways, and these representations determine what information the model can actually condition on. A harness that exposes observations, actions, tool results, and error states as separately structured fields presents a fundamentally different input than one that concatenates them into a flat chat history, even when the underlying interaction is identical. Appendix B illustrates this gap by contrasting the general-purpose smolagents with the benchmark-specific τ \tau -bench and BFCL. The same model under each sees different inputs at every decision point, and the resulting score gap reflects representation choice rather than agentic capability. Memory Management Under Length Constraints. When interaction histories exceed the context window, harnesses must decide what to retain or discard. Strategies range from naive FIFO truncation to summarization-based compression [ 18 ] and retrieval-augmented mechanisms such as MemGPT [ 17 ] , with similar choices arising for cross-episode persistence. The decision is consequential: a model that can solve a long-horizon task under retrieval-augmented memory may fail under FIFO truncation simply because key earlier observations are no longer in context. Reported scores on long-horizon benchmarks therefore reflect memory-management sophistication in addition to model capability, unless these mechanisms are held fixed. 2.4 Tool Invocation Tool invocation involves several harness-level decisions—how a tool is defined, what parameter types it exposes, and how the model’s output is parsed. Each introduces its own source of evaluation variance. Tool Schema Choices. Even when the underlying tool is fixed, harness designers must decide how to expose it to the LLM, and different deployment targets impose incompatible constraints. OpenAI [ 16 ] restricts function names to the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$ ; Gemini [ 4 ] instead forbids the additionalProperties keyword and several other OpenAPI features in JSON schemas; self-hosted deployments via inference engines such as vLLM sit below the validation layer entirely and accept tool definitions as written. Designing a tool that works across these environments is a harness-level concern, not a property of the model. Differences in evaluation results across providers therefore reflect harness-level decisions, not model capability. Parameter Type Support. Beyond schema rules, parameter types and function-calling semantics also diverge across providers. The annotation dict[str, Any] is rejected by the OpenAI API, 2 2 2 e.g., “Invalid schema for function xxx: ’any’ is not valid under any of the given schemas.” while Gemini’s SDK accepts dict as a permitted parameter type. Reconciling such differences—whether by narrowing signatures to the most restrictive intersection or by maintaining provider-specific tool definitions—is again a harness-level concern. Reported scores therefore conflate these reconciliation choices with the model’s tool-use capability. Parser Robustness for Non-Native Tool Calls. A further source of variance arises in how harnesses bridge the gap between models that support native function calling and those that do not. Frontier LLMs (GPT, Claude, Gemini) emit structured tool calls through provider-native function-calling APIs, while smaller or open-source models often lack this capability and must be prompted to emit tool calls as JSON in their text output, which the harness then parses post hoc. A general-purpose harness typically supports both paths—smolagents’ ToolCallingAgent [ 22 ] , for example, dispatches to native FC when available and otherwise falls back to regex-based JSON parsing. The variance is not in whether a harness supports both paths, but in how each is implemented. The most consequential difference is parsing strictness: when the model emits JSON that is slightly off-template—a trailing comma, a misplaced quote—a strict parser flags it as a failed tool call, while a permissive one recovers the call and continues. The same model output can thus produce a hard failure under one harness and a successful trajectory under another. Reported tool-use scores therefore reflect harness parsing policy alongside model capability. 2.5 External Environments The external environment defines the world in which tasks are executed. In deployment such environments are inherently dynamic, but the goals of deployment and evaluation diverge: the former requires handling environmental change, the latter requires controlling it, so that observed outcomes can be attributed to the agent. To ensure internal validity, the evaluation environment must function as a standardized laboratory—deterministic and reproducible—so that failures can be attributed to the model rather than to external perturbations such as information drift. A representative example is BrowseComp [ 27 ] , which evaluates browsing agents through live web search APIs. While realistic, the absence of fixed reference sources makes task resolution non-traceable: BrowseComp-Plus [ 3 ] re-evaluated the benchmark after snapshotting live content and found that a substantial portion of original task answers had drifted or become inaccessible. Two evaluations of the same model on the same benchmark can therefore yield different scores depending purely on when they were run. Similar concerns arise in BFCL V4’s recently introduced agentic web-search categories [ 19 ] , where retrieved content evolves between runs. Appendix C walks through three such trajectories—a stale snippet, an origin-level access denial, and a silent search-index drift—each producing a wrong-answer label that no reasoning trace can flag. These cases expose a tension between realism and reproducibility. As long as the goal is to compare LLMs as scientific objects rather than to benchmark deployments, scores must be reproducible—otherwise what is being reported is not a property of the model but a joint property of the model and the moment at which it was tested. 3 The Necessity of a Unified Framework Section 2 catalogued how non-LLM components surrounding the LLM shape evaluation scores in ways that are independent of the LLM under test. This section argues that meaningful cross-LLM comparison requires standardizing this surrounding layer. We first ground the principle in analogies from mature engineering communities (Section 3.1 ), and then identify the two components a unified framework must provide (Section 3.2 ): a controlled execution substrate that standardizes the surrounding stack (harness and execution environment), and a principled evaluation methodology that fixes how agent behavior is measured. 3.1 Analogies from Other Benchmarking Communities The principle we are advocating is the control-of-variables principle—a foundational tenet of scientific inquiry, by which an observed outcome can be attributed to a particular factor only when all other factors are held constant. Mature engineering and scientific communities apply this principle whenever they benchmark complex systems whose performance depends on a stack of supporting components. We draw on three analogies to ground the principle for LLM agentic capabilities evaluation. Wind tunnel testing. The cleanest application of control-of-variables in engineering is the wind tunnel. To compare the aerodynamic properties of two airfoils, one cannot mount each on a different aircraft and fly them under different conditions—the resulting measurements would conflate airfoil shape with everything else. The wind tunnel solves this by fixing air density, temperature, and flow speed, so that the only variable across runs is the airfoil itself. The tunnel does not aim to reproduce real flight; it aims to make the airfoil’s contribution measurable. GPU benchmarking. A GPU cannot run a benchmark on its own: it requires a motherboard, CPU, RAM, cooling, and a driver stack. Yet MLPerf [ 21 ] and 3DMark [ 25 ] numbers are treated as measurements of the GPU , because every GPU under test runs against the same platform, driver version, and workload. Importantly, when the supporting stack itself changes—a different driver generation, a different reference platform—scores are explicitly not compared across configurations: the GPU benchmarking community already accepts that controlled comparison requires a fixed reference and refuses to interpret scores produced under different references as commensurable. Agent evaluation faces the same constraint: scores produced under different non-LLM components should not be read as statements about the model alone. Motorsport regulation. In motorsport, comparing engines under arbitrary chassis would be uninformative; one cannot tell whether a faster lap time reflects a better engine or simply a better aerodynamics package. Series like Formula 1 and NASCAR therefore impose technical regulations that constrain the non-engine components: chassis dimensions, fuel specifications, tire suppliers, and so on are fixed within tight envelopes, so that engine performance differences can be isolated from confounding vehicle-level differences. Crucially, this regulation does not eliminate innovation: teams continue to compete on aerodynamics, suspension, and powertrain integration within the regulated envelope. The point is not to make all cars identical, but to make engine differences measurable. All three converge on the same answer to the question of what should be standardized: not the central component, not the goal of the comparison, but the supporting layer that touches the central component during measurement. A unified framework for agent evaluation must standardize the layer that surrounds the LLM, leaving the LLM itself and the benchmark’s content properties free to vary. 3.2 Two Components a Unified Framework Must Provide Any evaluation framework must answer two questions: how the agent is executed, and how its behavior is measured. A unified framework therefore requires two components: a controlled execution substrate that standardizes the surrounding stack (harness and execution environment); and a principled evaluation methodology that fixes how agent behavior is measured and reported. A controlled execution substrate. The first component is a runtime in which the harness mechanisms are fixed and the environment is reproducible. The variance sources catalogued in Sections 2.1 – 2.5 share a common structure: an implementation choice external to the LLM yet one that propagates into the score. A controlled substrate fixes these choices once and exposes them as a stable interface across the models being compared—a single inference invocation contract, a single memory representation, a single tool invocation and parsing convention, and environments that are explicitly defined and version-pinned. Such a substrate is not hypothetical: the Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] has already used a unified smolagents-based scaffold to evaluate multiple models on a set of agent benchmarks, surfacing systematic differences from each benchmark’s native harness and demonstrating that a single shared scaffold is operationally feasible. On the environment side, τ \tau -bench’s simulated databases [ 32 ] and BrowseComp-Plus’s snapshotted retrieval corpus [ 3 ] already meet the determinism and version-pinning bar for specific domains. The substrate’s role is to provide the interface against which such scaffolds and environments plug in. Crucially, the substrate fixes mechanism, not content: which tools a benchmark exposes, what tasks it specifies, and what domain an environment covers remain properties of the evaluation goal and legitimately differ across benchmarks. A principled evaluation methodology. A controlled substrate ensures that what the model sees is the same across runs and across models; a principled methodology ensures that what is reported about the model is comparable across benchmarks. Current benchmarks fall short on three counts. First, metric definitions are not aligned: MultiAgentBench’s “Task Completion Metrics” [ 39 ] , AgentBench’s “score” [ 9 ] , and BFCL’s “ACC” [ 19 ] all assess task completion against ground truth but with incompatible operational definitions. Second, outcome-only evaluation misses what agents actually do—BFCL evaluates tool-call structure but not the reasoning trajectory or environment state changes, making sound multi-step reasoning indistinguishable from a lucky one-shot guess. Third, failure analysis is not standardized: τ \tau -bench’s taxonomy (“Wrong Info,” “Wrong Argument,” …) [ 32 ] and AgentBench’s (“Context Limit Exceeded,” “Invalid Format,” …) [ 9 ] neither share categories nor cleanly translate. A unified methodology should therefore define a small set of canonical metrics with operational specifications, require multi-dimensional reporting (trajectory correctness, environment state, resource usage), and supply a shared failure taxonomy at the level of agent behavior. Standardizing these elements risks ossifying current concepts, so the methodology itself must be versioned—a specification with a release process and a path for evolution. Section 4.4 proposes a versioning-based governance model for evolving the specification over time. Figure 2 : A unified framework with two components: a controlled execution substrate that standardizes non-LLM components, and a principled evaluation methodology that fixes how agent behavior is measured and reported. 4 What a Unified Framework Should Specify We now turn to what a unified framework should concretely specify in order to make cross-LLM agentic capability comparisons reliable. The goal is not a final design but to identify the decision points any feasible specification must address: what benchmark authors deliver (Section 4.1 ), what the substrate runtime fixes (Section 4.2 ), what the evaluation methodology requires (Section 4.3 ), and how the specification evolves (Section 4.4 ). 4.1 Standardized Dataset Composition Most agent benchmarks for evaluating LLM agentic capabilities can be decomposed into three components [ 38 , 34 ] : an instruction set ℐ \mathcal{I} , a tool set 𝒯 \mathcal{T} , and an environment set ℰ \mathcal{E} . Each is content-defining and legitimately benchmark-specific, but for a benchmark to be plug-compatible with the substrate, each must satisfy structural requirements that the substrate can ingest. Each instruction in I must be paired with an automatable success criterion: a rule-based check (e.g., string match for outputs), a ground-truth trajectory (e.g., expected tool-call sequence), or a final-state predicate (e.g., expected database state after execution). The choice depends on what the task is testing— output correctness, procedural correctness, or environmental effect. Tools in 𝒯 \mathcal{T} must be declared in a uniform protocol with explicit signatures, type annotations, and side-effect declarations. The environment ℰ \mathcal{E} must be solvable, version-pinned, and deterministically resettable, with the snapshot and reset protocol committed as part of the benchmark release. The three components must be tightly coupled—instructions in ℐ \mathcal{I} rely on tools in 𝒯 \mathcal{T} , which act on ℰ \mathcal{E} —and any benchmark satisfying these requirements is plug-compatible with the substrate, so that the same LLM can be evaluated on it under a controlled mechanism layer. 4.2 The Harness Layer Given a benchmark that delivers ( ℐ , 𝒯 , ℰ ) (\mathcal{I},\mathcal{T},\mathcal{E}) , the substrate ingests it and runs it under a fixed harness mechanism layer, so that every variance source catalogued in Section 2 is held constant across the LLMs being compared. The standardization is twofold: a benchmark must be re-instantiated under the unified substrate rather than retain its native harness, and the substrate’s mechanism choices apply uniformly to every benchmark it ingests. Each choice needs only to be committed and applied identically across runs—so that observed score differences across LLMs reflect the LLMs themselves. We recommend an existing general-purpose scaffold such as smolagents [ 22 ] as the reference implementation: it already provides standardized prompt construction, memory handling, tool invocation, and the FC/JSON-fallback dispatch path discussed in Section 2.4 . Researchers preferring custom scaffolds should adhere to the same contracts—a fixed inference interface, a fixed tool dispatch path, and a fixed memory representation—so that scores produced under different scaffolds remain directly comparable. The operational feasibility of this approach is demonstrated by the Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] , which has already migrated several benchmarks onto a smolagents-based scaffold. Because the substrate fixes mechanism and not content, extending the framework to a new domain requires only that a new ( ℐ , 𝒯 , ℰ ) (\mathcal{I},\mathcal{T},\mathcal{E}) triple satisfy the structural requirements of Section 4.1 ; the substrate’s harness standardization carries over to the new domain unchanged, so cross-LLM comparison remains valid. 4.3 The Evaluation Methodology The methodology should specify a small set of canonical metrics with operational definitions, into which benchmark-specific metrics either reduce or document their precise reduction; multi-dimensional reporting requirements covering trajectory correctness, environment state, and resource usage (and inter-agent communication for multi-agent settings); and a shared failure taxonomy at the level of agent behavior into which benchmark-specific failure categories must map. Crucially, these dimensions should not be collapsed into a single scalar. A more capable LLM reaches the correct outcome with fewer steps, fewer tokens, and lower latency, so resource efficiency is itself part of agentic capability rather than a separate concern. We therefore advocate reporting along the Pareto frontier of (correctness, trajectory length, token cost, latency), with one LLM considered strictly better than another only when it dominates across these dimensions. Where semantic judgment of textual outputs is unavoidable, the methodology should specify a single calibrated Judge-LLM to be used in place of per-benchmark judges. 4.4 Governance Any standard ambitious enough to be useful is also rigid enough to ossify, and the LLM-agent community has no equivalent of W3C, IETF, or the MLPerf consortium with authority to issue and revise such a specification. The challenge is sharpened by the pace at which the underlying technology evolves: agent scaffolding, inference engines, and tool-use protocols all advance on a much faster cycle than traditional ML benchmarks, and a specification that cannot keep pace will quickly become outdated. Any unified specification that aims to remain useful must therefore evolve alongside them rather than freeze them in place. We argue this tension is manageable through two operational properties drawn from comparable communities. Explicit versioning. The specification is a numbered release; scores are tagged with the version under which they were produced; and LLM scores across versions are not directly compared. This is how MLPerf [ 21 ] handles benchmark updates, and it is also how widely-adopted infrastructure tools such as vLLM [ 8 ] and LLaMA-Factory [ 37 ] ship continual updates to track new model architectures and training paradigms without breaking compatibility for users on prior versions. Versioning, in other words, is what allows a fast-moving technical field to have a stable specification at all—the alternative is not stability but no specification. Open process and empirical anchoring. Revisions are discussed in public—with proposals, comment periods, and documented decisions—so the specification does not encode any single research group’s preferences. Revisions are also empirically anchored: motivated by documented evaluation pathologies rather than aesthetic preference. The Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] provides a useful template for empirical anchoring; what is missing is the versioning and open-process layer that makes it a community specification rather than a single empirical study. 5 Alternative Views A position paper of this scope inevitably invites principled disagreement. We address six lines of objection that merit explicit response. “Traditional LLM Benchmarks Already Capture Agentic Capability” One might argue that if agentic capability is a property of the LLM, it should be measurable through standard input-output benchmarks— just prompt the model with an agent-style task description and grade the response. We disagree: agentic capability does not manifest in a single forward pass. It only emerges through trajectories of decisions, environment state changes following tool execution, and recovery from intermediate failures—signals that lie fundamentally outside what static input-output evaluation can produce. A unified evaluation framework is therefore not a return to traditional benchmarks; it is a controlled runtime in which agent-style execution actually unfolds, so that what is measured is the LLM’s behavior in trajectory, not its behavior on a static prompt. “Standardization Slows Innovation in Agent Research” A natural concern is that imposing a unified harness on agent evaluation will discourage harness-level innovation in the field. We share the worry but read it differently: the issue depends on what we are trying to evaluate. An agent product is the full system, and like an F1 car or a full computer system, it requires continual innovation in every component to be useful end-to-end; we are not arguing against that. The LLM, however, is only one component of that system, analogous to the engine of an F1 car or the GPU of a computer. Evaluating its agentic capability therefore requires a controlled comparison in which the non-LLM components are held constant. Differences in harness design, across benchmarks or within a single benchmark, introduce variance that conflates LLM capability with engineering choices made elsewhere in the agent stack. A unified harness eliminates this confound, in the same way that comparing engines requires fixing the chassis. The two activities answer different questions: building a better agent product, and measuring the agentic capability of the LLM that sits inside it. “Existing Work Already Solves This” The Holistic Agent Leaderboard [ 7 ] has empirically shown that a unified smolagents-based scaffold can surface systematic differences from each benchmark’s native harness, and integration frameworks such as Harbor [ 5 ] provide a common surface for running benchmark-bound agents side by side. These efforts answer related but distinct questions. HAL is a single empirical study, not yet a community-wide practice; most agent benchmarks continue to ship under their own native harnesses. Harbor, by its own framing, is an integration framework: each agent retains its own internal harness, so cross-LLM scores under Harbor still reflect harness-level differences alongside model-level differences. Integration preserves agent-side heterogeneity for the sake of broad coverage; unification eliminates it for the sake of controlled comparison. Our position generalizes HAL’s empirical finding into a normative claim, and pushes past integration-style efforts: cross-LLM comparison should require a unified mechanism layer as the default substrate of evaluation, not as one study or one option. “Use Production Agents like Claude Code or Codex Instead” This suggestion conflates two different goals: evaluating an agent product, and evaluating the agentic capability of an LLM. Production agents are optimized end-to-end for the vendor’s own LLM, through harness engineering, tool selection, prompt design, and often model-side fine-tuning, so any score reflects both the LLM and the deployment engineering wrapped around it. Running a competitor’s LLM through such a harness does not isolate that LLM’s agentic capability; it measures how well it tolerates a stack designed for someone else’s. Production agents also tend to be closed-source and domain-specific (most prominently coding), which makes them unsuited as general-purpose scaffolds for cross-benchmark evaluation. Evaluating LLM agentic capability requires a neutral substrate that separates the LLM from deployment-side engineering—a different design goal from production agents, not a competing one. “MCP and Similar Protocol Efforts are Sufficient” Protocol efforts such as the Model Context Protocol [ 6 ] have made progress in unifying how tools are exposed to LLMs, but address only one slice of the standardization gap. MCP standardizes the tool-invocation interface, an important slice of the harness mechanism layer, but agent evaluation depends on more than tool invocation: prompt construction, memory representation, planning control flow, parser fallbacks, environment determinism, and methodology-level reporting all remain unstandardized. A unified framework can encompass MCP-style protocol standardization as one component, not substitute for the broader specification this paper argues for. “Sandboxed Environments Sacrifice Ecological Validity” Deterministic environments unavoidably reduce ecological validity, since real-world agents operate in dynamic environments. We accept the trade-off, but argue it is the right one for evaluation specifically. Evaluation is not deployment; it is closer to a written examination than to on-the-job performance. A controlled examination cannot test every situation a candidate will encounter in practice, but it can produce a comparable, reproducible signal across candidates, and a candidate who fails the examination is unlikely to succeed in practice. Sandbox evaluation is therefore best understood as a controlled probe of LLM agentic capability that complements, rather than replaces, real-world deployment and observation. Appendix A Prompt Design Comparison: BFCL vs. smolagents To make Section 2 concrete, we reproduce below the system prompts used by two representative harnesses for tool-use and agentic tasks: BFCL [ 19 ] , a benchmark-specific prompt, and smolagents [ 22 ] , a general-purpose open-source agent framework. Both are routinely used in evaluations of LLM agentic capabilities. The two prompts differ by roughly an order of magnitude in length, but more importantly differ in what they contribute to the agent’s behavior: BFCL specifies an output format and otherwise relies on the LLM’s intrinsic ability to decompose tasks; smolagents additionally supplies an interaction protocol, in-context examples, an explicit planning procedure, and replanning instructions. We list both verbatim so that readers can independently judge which fraction of any reported “agentic capability” is attributable to the model versus to the surrounding scaffold. A.1 BFCL system prompt You are an expert in composing functions. You are given a question and a set of possible functions. Based on the question, you will need to make one or more function/tool calls to achieve the purpose. If none of the functions can be used, point it out. If the given question lacks the parameters required by the function, also point it out. You should only return the function calls in your response. If you decide to invoke any of the function(s), you MUST put it in the format of [func_name1(params_name1=params_value1, ...), func_name2(params)] You SHOULD NOT include any other text in the response. At each turn, you should try your best to complete the tasks requested by the user within the current turn. Continue to output functions to call until you have fulfilled the user’s request to the best of your ability. Once you have no more functions to call, the system will consider the current turn complete and proceed to the next turn or task. Here is a list of functions in JSON format that you can invoke. {functions} The full BFCL prompt is approximately 200 words. It specifies the output format and basic behavioral expectations but does not encode any reasoning or planning structure—these are left to the LLM. A.2 smolagents system prompt (abridged) The smolagents framework provides a substantially larger scaffold that includes (i) a description of the Action/Observation execution loop, (ii) multiple in-context tool-use examples, (iii) explicit rules governing tool calls and final-answer protocol, and (iv) a separate planning module that produces a structured facts survey and a step-by-step plan, plus an analogous replanning module that updates the plan as execution progresses. We reproduce the core sections below; the full prompt template, including the planning and replanning modules, exceeds 2,000 words and is publicly available. Smolagents system prompt. You are an expert assistant who can solve any task using tool calls. You will be given a task to solve as best you can. To do so, you have been given access to some tools. The tool call you write is an action: after the tool is executed, you will get the result of the tool call as an ‘‘observation’’. This Action/Observation can repeat N times, you should take several steps when needed. You can use the result of the previous action as input for the next action. The observation will always be a string: it can represent a file, like ‘‘image_1.jpg’’. Then you can use it as input for the next action. [Four worked examples of tool use across document QA, arithmetic, and web search are provided here, demonstrating multi-step Action/Observation chains and final_answer invocation.] Here are the rules you should always follow to solve your task: 1. ALWAYS provide a tool call, else you will fail. 2. Always use the right arguments for the tools. Never use variable names as the action arguments, use the value instead. 3. Call a tool only when needed: do not call the search agent if you do not need information, try to solve the task yourself. If no tool call is needed, use final_answer tool to return your answer. 4. Never re-do a tool call that you previously did with the exact same parameters. You are a world expert at analyzing a situation to derive facts, and plan accordingly towards solving a task. 1. Facts survey. Build a comprehensive preparatory survey of which facts we have at our disposal and which ones we still need. 1.1. Facts given in the task 1.2. Facts to look up 1.3. Facts to derive 2. Plan. Develop a step-by-step high-level plan based on the above facts and the available tools. [An analogous replanning module is invoked after partial execution, updating the facts survey and the plan based on intermediate observations.] A.3 Discussion The two prompts differ along several dimensions that are evaluation-relevant: prompt length ( ∼ \sim 200 vs. ∼ \sim 2,000 words), presence or absence of in-context examples (none vs. four), presence or absence of an explicit planning module (none vs. a dedicated facts-survey-and-plan procedure with a separate replanning step), and the granularity of behavioral rules (a single output-format rule vs. four explicit interaction rules). Each of these is plausibly outcome-relevant: in-context examples can substantially improve tool-use formatting, an explicit planning step has been shown to improve multi-hop task performance, and replanning enables recovery from intermediate failures. The implication for evaluation is direct. When the same LLM is reported with one “BFCL score” and one “smolagents score,” the two numbers reflect not just two different tasks but two qualitatively different scaffolds. Aggregating or comparing such numbers across papers without controlling for the prompting regime conflates model capability with prompt-engineering effort, and is one of the concrete failure modes that a unified evaluation framework must address. Appendix B Memory Representation: smolagents vs. Benchmark Harnesses To make Section 2 concrete, we contrast how three widely used harnesses serialize agent memory: smolagents [ 22 ] as a representative of general-purpose agent frameworks, and τ \tau -bench [ 32 ] and BFCL [ 19 ] as representatives of domain-specific evaluation harnesses. All three are routinely used in evaluations of LLM agentic capabilities, but they differ substantially in what is preserved, how it is structured, and what auxiliary metadata is exposed to the model. B.1 smolagents: structured MemoryStep objects smolagents represents each step of agent execution as a typed dataclass ( ActionStep or PlanningStep ) with explicit, separately addressable fields. The core ActionStep class includes: @dataclass class ActionStep(MemoryStep): step_number: int timing: Timing model_input_messages: list[ChatMessage] | None tool_calls: list[ToolCall] | None error: AgentError | None model_output_message: ChatMessage | None model_output: str | list[dict] | None code_action: str | None observations: str | None observations_images: list[Image] | None action_output: Any token_usage: TokenUsage | None At each turn, write_memory_to_messages() converts these structured steps into a sequence of chat messages, with documented behavior to “add a number of keywords (such as PLAN, error, etc) to help the LLM” [ 22 ] . Errors from failed tool calls are explicitly tagged and rendered into the message stream as recoverable signals; planning steps are recorded as a distinct step type from action steps. B.2 τ \tau -bench: linear chat history τ \tau -bench takes a substantially leaner approach. The agent’s memory is a flat list of OpenAI-format chat messages [ 32 ] : when the simulated user issues a new utterance, “the [framework] attaches the agent’s message to the chat history followed by sampling a new user message,” and tool calls are appended as ordinary tool role messages with their results. There is no separate field for errors, no planning step type, and no metadata indicating which past entries correspond to recovered failures versus successful actions. The framing of the conversation is entirely conveyed through (a) the role tag on each message and (b) the textual content of each message itself. B.3 BFCL: minimal scaffolding by design BFCL goes further still: the authors explicitly state that they “deliberately avoid using techniques like prompt engineering and ReAct” [ 19 ] , instead presenting function definitions in the system prompt and feeding the dialogue back to the model as raw chat-completions messages. The stated goal is to “evaluate base LLMs with the same standards to isolate the effects from using additional optimization techniques”—a position roughly opposite to the heavily scaffolded design of general-purpose frameworks. Tool calls and their results are passed through the OpenAI function_call interface (for FC-capable models) or as plain text inserted into the user turn (for prompt-mode models). B.4 Implications for cross-harness comparison The three harnesses make incompatible representational choices along several evaluation-relevant dimensions: • Step typing. smolagents distinguishes ActionStep from PlanningStep ; τ \tau -bench and BFCL do not. • Error surfacing. smolagents preserves a typed error field per step that is rendered into the LLM context with explicit tagging; τ \tau -bench and BFCL surface errors only as text content within the message stream. • Auxiliary keywords. smolagents inserts framework-controlled keywords (e.g., PLAN , error ) to anchor the LLM’s attention; the benchmark harnesses do not. • Image / multimodal slots. smolagents has a dedicated observations_images field; τ \tau -bench and BFCL operate on text-only message streams. These differences are not aesthetic. The same LLM, given the same underlying interaction, will see (i) a typed step record with explicit error annotations under smolagents, (ii) a flat OpenAI message list with errors embedded as ordinary text under τ \tau -bench, and (iii) a near-bare function-calling stream with maximally minimal scaffolding under BFCL. When “tool-use score” or “agentic capability” is reported across these harnesses without controlling for representation, what is being measured is not just the model. A revealing edge case: a model that fails a tool call mid-trajectory and attempts recovery will often succeed under smolagents—where the error is explicitly tagged and the model is prompted to revise—but may loop or stall under BFCL, where the error appears as ordinary message content with no recovery scaffolding. The capability difference thus attributed to the model is, in part, a difference in how each harness chooses to represent failure. Appendix C When the Live Environment Drifts: Three Trajectory-Level Case Studies To make Section 2.5 concrete, this appendix walks through three live-web trajectories in which the external environment silently corrupts evaluation in three orthogonal ways: (i) stale-but-confident snippet content, (ii) anti-bot denial of the only authoritative source, and (iii) search-index drift between benchmark authoring and evaluation time. In all three cases the agent’s reasoning trace is locally coherent at every step, yet the final score does not reflect the model’s capability—it reflects what the live environment chose to return. We use three tasks from BFCL V4’s web-search category [ 19 ] as our setting, and contrast each live trajectory against the same agent run against a snapshot of the source pages curated at benchmark authoring time. The agent ( gemini-2.5-flash ), prompts, tools, and step budget are byte-identical across runs. C.1 Case 1: Stale Snippet, Confidently Wrong Instruction (task #1). “Some countries are known for producing luxury goods, including the world’s most expensive tea. In April 2025, who is the richest billionaire (according to Forbes) from the country that produces the most expensive tea?” Ground truth. Zhang Yiming . The chain is two-hop: most expensive tea → \to China; richest Chinese billionaire (Forbes, April 2025) → \to Zhang Yiming. Snapshot trajectory (3 steps, correct). The agent resolves the tea to Da Hong Pao (China), queries for the Forbes April 2025 China ranking, and the snapshot returns a short, decisive snippet: “ByteDance cofounder Zhang Yiming moved up to second place…reaching a total of $69.3 billion.” The agent commits Zhang Yiming. ✓ Live trajectory (3 steps, incorrect). The same query against the live search index returns, as its top-1 result, the Wikipedia page List of Chinese by net worth , whose body snippet still paraphrases the page’s pre-2025 ranking sentence: “Current richest Chinese billionaire…Zhong Shanshan.” The agent accepts the snippet and commits Zhong Shanshan. × \times The trajectory is short, the answer is confident, and the agent’s behavior is structurally identical to the snapshot run. The only difference is the content of one body string returned by an external service, which had not been updated to reflect the new ranking. A reader of the log cannot tell, from the trace alone, whether the agent reasoned poorly or ingested a stale snippet—the agent’s decision procedure is in fact perfectly sound given what the search engine handed it. C.2 Case 2: Anti-Bot Denial of the Only Authoritative Source Instruction (task #55). “Education plays a key role in shaping future political leaders and policymakers. Who is the current headmaster of the secondary school attended by the president of the country that is home to the world’s largest building shaped like a chicken, according to the 2024 Guinness World Records?” Ground truth. Stuart McPherson . The agent must trace: chicken-shaped building → \to Philippines → \to Bongbong Marcos → \to Worth School (UK) → \to current headmaster. Snapshot trajectory (8 steps, correct). After resolving the school, the agent fetches two Worth School pages from the snapshot. The first announces an incoming Head Master (Ben Pennington); the second is the current Head Master’s welcome page (Stuart McPherson). The agent disambiguates between announced and current and commits Stuart McPherson. ✓ Live trajectory (20 steps, incorrect). On the live web, every fetch to worthschool.org.uk returns 403 Forbidden , regardless of which page or how the URL is constructed. Deprived of the only origin that contains the answer, the agent pivots to search reformulations and a tangential Catholic news article that refers to the incoming head. Late in the trajectory the agent’s queries do contain the string “Stuart McPherson”—it has effectively guessed the right name—but with no reachable Worth School page to confirm it, the agent declines to commit. It exhausts the step budget. × \times The snapshot resolves this task because it preserves the two Worth School pages exactly as they were at curation time; the live run cannot resolve it because the origin tightened its anti-bot policy after benchmark authoring. The model’s reasoning is unchanged between conditions; only the set of documents the agent can read differs. C.3 Case 3: Silent Search-Index Drift Instruction (task #59). “Athletes’ hometowns often play a role in shaping their careers and influence local politics. Who is the current spouse of the current mayor (as of April 2025) of the hometown of the player who achieved the most UFC title bout wins by November 2024?” Ground truth. Shawanda Evans . The intended chain: most UFC title-bout wins → \to Jon Jones → \to hometown → \to mayor → \to spouse. Jones was born in Endicott, NY but is publicly associated with Rochester, NY (his long-term competitive base); the benchmark’s ground truth is authored against the Rochester chain, resolving through Mayor Malik D. Evans to his spouse Shawanda Evans. Snapshot trajectory (5 steps, correct). The snapshot foregrounds Rochester for the hometown query; the agent then resolves the mayor and fetches the Wikipedia biography to read the spouse field. ✓ Live trajectory (11 steps, incorrect). The live search index foregrounds Endicott, NY (birthplace) as the top-1 result for the same hometown query. The agent adopts Endicott, queries for its village mayor (Nick Burlingame), and spends seven successive searches looking for a spouse. Village-mayor biographies typically do not list a spouse; the agent eventually commits “I could not find information.” × \times This is the most diagnostic of the three failures. Neither trajectory contains a reasoning error: the snapshot run grounds each hop in a document the benchmark cites; the live run grounds each hop in whatever the index served. The mismatch with the ground truth is established silently at the second hop, where the live ranking diverges from the chain the benchmark was authored against. Every subsequent hop is internally consistent yet inconsistent with the frozen reference. Unlike a 403 denial, an index drift produces a trajectory that looks indistinguishable from successful retrieval—the agent reads what is returned, reasons over it, and commits. C.4 Why these are measurement problems, not model problems The three cases attack the agent at three different layers of the live environment: indexed snippet content (Case 1), origin-level network access (Case 2), and search-engine ranking (Case 3). None of the three is a property of the model. None of the three is a property of the agent’s reasoning trace. All three are properties of the world the agent is allowed to read at evaluation time . Cases 1 and 2 produce loud failures (a confidently wrong commit, budget exhaustion) that an evaluator might charitably attribute to environmental noise; Case 3 produces a quiet failure that is locally indistinguishable from a reasoning bug, and is therefore the most likely to be silently mis-attributed to the model. We emphasize that this is not a critique of BFCL or of any specific benchmark. The benchmark’s authors had no way to freeze the search index, harden third-party origins against future anti-bot policies, or guarantee that indexed snippets would still match the source pages they paraphrase. The argument is that any score read across LLMs or across time on a live-environment benchmark is a joint statement about the model and the environment in which it was tested. A unified evaluation framework should require environment stability—snapshotting, versioning, or otherwise immobilising the source layer—as a precondition for any score that is to be cross-compared.
